title: Loop Engineer
hook: Employ a mentor. Master the agent loop.
fantasy: Employ an NPC mentor who teaches agentic software development across SDLC and SDLR through visible loop engineering.
first20Seconds: Patch deliberately follows an underspecified feature request, delivers the wrong cube, and asks what done should look like before the next SDLC and SDLR loop.
gameLoop: Define the feature and observable result
Plan and delegate bounded specialist jobs
desktopControls: Click cards, drag to stations, Space to run or pause.
mobileControls: Tap-select, tap-place, one-finger orbit, large Run button.
xrControls: Ray-select or grab cards, place at waist-high stations, press Run.
comfort: Seated tabletop; no locomotion; captions, undo, and readable evidence.
signatureVisual: A hired mentor and player route a red task cube between a glowing SDLC timeline and an interlocking SDLR feedback loop while specialist agents branch onto bounded tracks.
whyDifferent: The employee teaches the employer to carry a feature across the software development life cycle while using SDLR, bounded ownership, tests, feedback, and evidence-driven loops instead of programming worker steps.
nearestComparable: Human Resource Machine, 7 Billion Humans, and while True: learn().
originalityRisk: Medium - familiar worker metaphors, but a distinct mentor-employer relationship and evidence loop.
hackathonScope: One conveyor scenario, two agent roles, one bad run, and one corrected run.
technicalRisk: It may feel like card sorting unless every decision changes visible behavior within seconds.

title: Echo Cartographer
hook: Map darkness by triangulating visible echoes.
fantasy: Reveal an invisible cave with limited pulses, then guide a luminous moth through the map you inferred.
first20Seconds: One pulse paints fading wall returns; a second pulse resolves a doorway where the echoes overlap.
desktopControls: Aim with mouse, click to pulse, click returns to pin.
mobileControls: Drag to aim, tap pulse, tap returns and waypoint arrows.
xrControls: Point a tuning wand, trigger a pulse, touch or ray-pin returns.
comfort: Seated or standing; static viewpoints; every sound has geometry, captions, and optional haptics.
signatureVisual: Concentric light rings strike unseen cave walls and leave a temporary constellation around a waiting cave moth.
whyDifferent: Pulses from multiple positions must become navigable geometry, not merely reveal a dark level.
nearestComparable: Scanner Sombre and knowledge-led exploration games.
originalityRisk: Medium - echolocation is recognizable; triangulation plus moth routing must dominate.
hackathonScope: Three chambers, six surfaces, one false echo, and one moth rescue.
technicalRisk: Spatial audio varies by device, so visual timing must fully carry the puzzle.

title: Gravity Loom
hook: Weave gravity threads into living constellations.
fantasy: Stretch temporary gravity threads so drifting star-seeds curve into a stable musical constellation.
first20Seconds: Connect two anchors and watch a star-seed bend immediately along a painted prediction arc.
desktopControls: Click anchor pairs, wheel tension, Space to simulate.
mobileControls: Tap anchor pairs, adjust a tension slider, tap timeline.
xrControls: Pinch or ray-select anchors; move hands apart or use a slider.
comfort: Fixed tabletop sky; no locomotion; slow particles and stepped reduced-motion arcs.
signatureVisual: Luminous threads bend pearl star-seeds into a constellation that begins to sing when stable.
whyDifferent: Gravity is a woven, adjustable field used to compose a stable pattern rather than launch objects through a general physics sandbox.
nearestComparable: Orbital trajectory and physics-routing puzzles.
originalityRisk: Low - the temporary thread metaphor and musical stability target are unusual.
hackathonScope: Three arrangements, one thread type, and one constellation payoff.
technicalRisk: Trajectories must remain deterministic and legible across frame rates.

title: Pocket Weather Bureau
hook: Forecast one island's beautiful impossible compromise.
fantasy: Run a tiny weather office where one pressure intervention must balance farms, surf, and a festival.
first20Seconds: Draw a high-to-low pressure path and watch clouds stream toward a thirsty village.
desktopControls: Drag front paths, wheel strength, click the forecast clock.
mobileControls: Finger-draw paths, turn a large strength dial, tap advance.
xrControls: Draw above the map or use a ray; turn a chunky time wheel.
comfort: Static island; gentle time-lapse; reduced motion replaces cloud flow with contours.
signatureVisual: A hand moves a blue pressure shape above an island as clouds bend toward fields and a festival blooms.
whyDifferent: The score rewards prediction accuracy and transparent public tradeoffs, not decoration or perfect weather.
nearestComparable: Compact management simulations and tabletop world toys such as Townscaper.
originalityRisk: Medium - the tabletop simulation is familiar; forecast accountability is the hook.
hackathonScope: One island, three settlements, two fronts, and one festival deadline.
technicalRisk: A simplified weather model can feel arbitrary unless arrows and before-after values explain it.

title: Choir Engine
hook: Conduct a factory until machinery finds harmony.
fantasy: Cue a singing machine's sections so musical timing becomes the production schedule.
first20Seconds: Cue the piston section on a glowing beat; the conveyor moves in rhythm while a mistimed cue squeaks.
desktopControls: Select sections with mouse; swipe or use keys for cue, hold, and cut.
mobileControls: Use large section pads and directional swipes.
xrControls: Conduct with a baton and trigger; ray buttons mirror gestures.
comfort: No locomotion; one-handed mode; generous timing; visual, haptic, and caption cues.
signatureVisual: Pistons, belts, and bells join a glowing groove while finished objects march out in harmony.
whyDifferent: The player schedules live machine sections through conducting instead of hitting incoming notes or programming conveyors.
nearestComparable: Maestro, rhythm games, and factory automation games.
originalityRisk: Medium - conducting is familiar; music as production control must stay central.
hackathonScope: One 60-second song, three sections, and three cue types.
technicalRisk: Audio and animation latency may differ on mobile and wireless XR.

title: Museum of Stillness
hook: Escape curators by becoming the perfect exhibit.
fantasy: Cross a museum as a runaway sculpture by impersonating nearby art whenever the curator looks.
first20Seconds: A spotlight sweeps toward you; choose a plinth pose and freeze until the curator approvingly passes.
desktopControls: Move between nodes, choose pose keys, hold Space to freeze.
mobileControls: Tap a node, tap a pose silhouette, hold Freeze.
xrControls: Approximate a simple pose or select its accessible ray icon.
comfort: Node movement only; seated pose set; no crouching; broad pose tolerance.
signatureVisual: A runaway sculpture freezes into an elegant silhouette as a curator beam crosses a midnight gallery.
whyDifferent: Stillness chooses a social disguise rather than stopping time or demanding athletic pose accuracy.
nearestComparable: Gaze-stealth, pose games, and SUPERHOT VR's stop-move readability.
originalityRisk: Medium - familiar ingredients, but the exhibit-identity stealth rule is distinct.
hackathonScope: One gallery, three poses, two gaze patterns, and one decoy.
technicalRisk: Pose recognition must remain optional and forgiving.

title: Ghostline Dispatcher
hook: Schedule ghost trains across overlapping decades.
fantasy: Route spectral trains through a station whose tracks and passengers exist in different remembered eras.
first20Seconds: Move one train to the 1984 layer so a ghost boards and passes through a modern obstruction.
desktopControls: Click routes, drag time tokens, wheel between era layers.
mobileControls: Tap endpoints and slots; use large vertical era tabs.
xrControls: Ray-select tracks, place time tokens, rotate an era dial.
comfort: Fixed overview; no train camera; reduced motion uses schematic markers.
signatureVisual: Translucent trains cross one station on three stacked decades while solid timetable tokens hold their paths apart.
whyDifferent: Scheduling a departure also selects which infrastructure and passengers exist, making time layer and route one decision.
nearestComparable: Mini Metro, Cosmic Express, and rail logistics puzzles.
originalityRisk: Medium - transit planning is crowded; temporal reservations must be unmistakable.
hackathonScope: Six track segments, three trains, five passenger groups, and three eras.
technicalRisk: Timetable complexity can overwhelm; expose one conflict and a clear why-blocked trace.

title: Reverse Archaeology
hook: Rewind artifacts to reconstruct their forgotten causes.
fantasy: Rewind a pristine future object through local damage states to discover who made it and why it mattered.
first20Seconds: Turn a time ring backward; a crack closes, ash rises into a lamp, and a clue reappears.
desktopControls: Orbit with mouse, wheel time, click evidence pins.
mobileControls: One-finger orbit, large time slider, tap evidence.
xrControls: Hold the artifact and turn a separate dial, or use rays.
comfort: Object-local motion only; slow rewind; dissolve option; no camera movement.
signatureVisual: A ruined room rises backward through translucent time layers around one artifact held at a glowing ring.
whyDifferent: The player tests causal predecessors by locally rewinding objects, rather than identifying people in frozen scenes or rewinding the whole world.
nearestComparable: Return of the Obra Dinn, artifact inspection, and timeline deduction games.
originalityRisk: Medium - evidence reconstruction is familiar; object-local causality is the separator.
hackathonScope: One room, four artifacts, one twist, and two valid interpretations.
technicalRisk: Authored transitions and story clarity are content-heavy.

title: Shadowwright
hook: Build 3D sculptures that cast playable shadows.
fantasy: Compose abstract forms whose shadows become bridges, shelters, and story props for a tiny projected traveler.
first20Seconds: Rotate a simple arch under a lamp; its shadow becomes a bridge and a traveler crosses it.
desktopControls: Drag rotation gizmos, wheel scale, switch object or light with keys.
mobileControls: Tap selection, use visible rotate and scale controls, turn a light dial.
xrControls: Directly grab or ray-manipulate forms with one-handed axis handles.
comfort: Fixed workbench; no locomotion; high-contrast outline and non-shadow preview modes.
signatureVisual: Simple cobalt forms under a lamp cast a crisp bridge where a tiny ink traveler walks.
whyDifferent: The shadow is a persistent functional world for a living character, not a silhouette-matching endpoint.
nearestComparable: Shadowmatic and light-and-shadow projection puzzles.
originalityRisk: Medium - shadow puzzles are established; functional story affordances must lead.
hackathonScope: One light, five primitives, and three traveler needs.
technicalRisk: Cross-device shadow scoring needs forgiving authored anchor regions.

title: Minute Garden
hook: Guide centuries with one change per generation.
fantasy: Steer a tiny ecosystem across centuries while species adapt beyond your direct control.
first20Seconds: Raise the water table; generations pass and roots shorten while a marsh insect arrives.
desktopControls: Click plots, wheel condition dials, Space to advance.
mobileControls: Tap plots, use large radial sliders, tap next generation.
xrControls: Point at plots and turn physical terrarium dials.
comfort: Static terrarium; stepped time option; no flicker or swarms near the face.
signatureVisual: A terrarium shifts through centuries as roots, insects, and flowers visibly adapt around one changed dial.
whyDifferent: The player makes one intervention per generation and optimizes resilience, not direct gardening output.
nearestComparable: Ecosystem simulations and cozy gardening games such as Garden of the Sea.
originalityRisk: Medium - familiar ecology, distinguished by limited intervention and lineage preservation.
hackathonScope: Three species, four traits, three generations, and two events.
technicalRisk: Emergence can seem random unless causal arrows explain every adaptation.

title: Portal Paparazzi
hook: Frame impossible spaces into one perfect photograph.
fantasy: Photograph celebrity creatures by aligning separate places inside a camera frame until composition becomes temporary truth.
first20Seconds: Align a rooftop moon inside a puddle portal and photograph a moon-whale breaching through the frame.
desktopControls: Aim with mouse, choose vantage keys, wheel zoom, click shutter.
mobileControls: Drag camera, tap vantage, pinch zoom, use a large shutter.
xrControls: Hold a virtual camera or use a fixed viewfinder panel.
comfort: Fixed tripods or teleport; stable viewfinder; no forced zoom; mobile gyro optional.
signatureVisual: A street puddle, rooftop moon, and breaching moon-whale align inside one luminous camera frame.
whyDifferent: Framed spaces become briefly contiguous for a timed creature performance instead of importing a photograph as general geometry.
nearestComparable: Viewfinder, Portal, and creature photography games.
originalityRisk: High - strong perspective and portal neighbors require a very specific photography loop.
hackathonScope: One plaza, one creature, three briefs, and three vantages.
technicalRisk: Portals and screenshot scoring may need a second-camera-plane illusion.

title: Gesture Linguist
hook: Speak an alien language drawn through space.
fantasy: Learn a language built from path, direction, depth, tempo, and distance, then repair a social misunderstanding.
first20Seconds: Copy an alien's slow inward triangle as hello; reverse it and provoke a comic goodbye.
desktopControls: Hold mouse to trace, wheel depth, choose tempo keys.
mobileControls: Finger-trace on depth layers and tap tempo buttons.
xrControls: Trace with controller or hand ray inside a reachable panel.
comfort: No locomotion; traces below eye level; one-handed panel mode; broad recognition tolerance.
signatureVisual: An alien and player exchange luminous paths whose direction and tempo transform the surrounding room.
whyDifferent: Meaning is compositional motion grammar acted out in context, not symbol substitution or precision gesture scoring.
nearestComparable: Chants of Sennaar and gesture-recognition games.
originalityRisk: Medium - language decoding is established; embodied syntax and comic interpretation differentiate it.
hackathonScope: Four glyphs, two grammar rules, and one three-turn conversation.
technicalRisk: Recognition frustration requires dimension-by-dimension feedback and tolerant matching.

title: Tiny Treaty Table
hook: Make promises that physically reshape the map.
fantasy: Broker survival among three miniature nations while every promise becomes infrastructure, access, or a lasting taboo.
first20Seconds: Promise river access to one nation; a bridge appears while the downstream orchard immediately dries.
desktopControls: Click nation, resource, and duration; preview and confirm.
mobileControls: Tap large treaty clauses and map overlays.
xrControls: Place chunky clause tiles into a treaty frame by touch or ray.
comfort: Seated tabletop; fixed map; snap rotation; no locomotion or time pressure.
signatureVisual: Three tiny nations surround a landscape where spoken promises rise as bridges, waterways, borders, and blocked roads.
whyDifferent: Diplomatic commitments become persistent spatial topology; the goal is coalition survival rather than conquest.
nearestComparable: Negotiation board games and Demeo-like miniature presentation.
originalityRisk: Low - visible promise topology is a clear mechanical identity.
hackathonScope: Three factions, two resources, three rounds, and six authored petitions.
technicalRisk: Text density must collapse into three readable clause icons and animated consequences.

title: Dream Assembly Line
hook: Program surreal dreams with readable if-then rules.
fantasy: Author a dream factory by arranging simple rules that transform alarm clocks, clouds, and impossible ingredients.
first20Seconds: Set an if-noisy-then-cloud rule and watch a clanging alarm clock become a quiet floating dream.
desktopControls: Click rule tiles, drag order, Space to run or pause.
mobileControls: Tap rules into slots and use move arrows.
xrControls: Snap rule blocks into a rack or use ray placement.
comfort: Stationary factory; moderate belt speed; reduced-motion step execution.
signatureVisual: Surreal objects pass through shape-coded machines and emerge as a bottled dream, with every matched rule visibly traced.
whyDifferent: It centers interpretable trait transformation and harmless creative exceptions, not worker programming or agent delegation.
nearestComparable: Human Resource Machine, 7 Billion Humans, and rule automation puzzles.
originalityRisk: High - programming-puzzle neighbors are strong; surreal transformation must lead.
hackathonScope: Six traits, six rule blocks, and two dream requests.
technicalRisk: Rules become opaque unless every object exposes its exact match and before-after traits.

title: The Unreliable Stage
hook: Improvise while the world changes the genre.
fantasy: Perform a tiny play while a mischievous stage manager mutates the set, genre, and emotional objective.
first20Seconds: Choose a confession response; the living room becomes a submarine and the same fact must still remain true.
desktopControls: Choose response keys and add delivery with mouse strokes.
mobileControls: Tap response cards and use simple swipe flourishes.
xrControls: Point to a response or perform an optional reachable gesture.
comfort: No locomotion; stable floor; scenery crossfades instead of spinning; seated director mode.
signatureVisual: One actor holds a sincere pose as the same stage flips from living room to submarine to western.
whyDifferent: Scoring rewards recovery, callbacks, and continuity under changing context rather than dialogue optimization or acting accuracy.
nearestComparable: Improvisational narrative games and The Under Presents.
originalityRisk: Medium - participatory theater exists; the persistent-fact rule creates focus.
hackathonScope: One three-beat scene, two set flips, and nine response cards.
technicalRisk: Branching content can explode; keep a fixed plot spine and vary context.

title: Message in a Moon
hook: Inherit, repair, and relay a tiny moon.
fantasy: Decode an inherited moon machine, repair one beat, and leave a new light-orbit-chime message for the next player.
first20Seconds: Play the inherited sequence, guess its intent, and reveal what the previous sender meant.
desktopControls: Click timeline cells, choose signals, drag beat order.
mobileControls: Tap large beats and use reorder arrows.
xrControls: Place light, orbit, and chime tokens around the moon.
comfort: Fixed diorama; bounded slow orbit; reduced-motion schematic mode.
signatureVisual: A pearl moon machine plays three glowing signal tokens while a lineage of prior repairs circles behind it.
whyDifferent: The artifact itself carries a constrained asynchronous conversation and visible lineage instead of text or drawings.
nearestComparable: Asynchronous message, collaborative toy, and relay-art games.
originalityRisk: Low - the fixed signal vocabulary and inherited machine form a clear niche.
hackathonScope: Three signal types, four intents, one seed, and a copy-paste share code.
technicalRisk: A public relay needs moderation; the demo should stay local or use share codes.

title: Second-Hand Sun
hook: Schedule sunlight to unlock one impossible room.
fantasy: Move a miniature sun with giant clock hands so objects wake, age, grow, and cast useful shadows at precise times.
first20Seconds: Turn the room to dawn; a flower opens and its shadow reveals a hidden keyhole.
desktopControls: Drag clock hands, click memory pins, step time with keys.
mobileControls: Use a large clock dial and tap memory pins.
xrControls: Turn reachable hands or use ray handles.
comfort: Fixed room; slow light transitions; no flashing, locomotion, or camera motion.
signatureVisual: Giant clock hands move a tiny sun around one room as a flower shadow opens a pearl keyhole.
whyDifferent: Time is a scheduled lighting resource with scarce state pins, not a rewind or recorded-clone mechanic.
nearestComparable: Clockwork rooms, light-and-shadow puzzles, and The Room VR.
originalityRisk: Medium - familiar time and light motifs, separated by discrete windows and memory pins.
hackathonScope: One room, four time states, three objects, and two pins.
technicalRisk: Lighting must use reliable authored presets instead of expensive dynamic simulation.

title: Invisible Zoo Keeper
hook: Care for creatures visible only through evidence.
fantasy: Diagnose and care for unseen animals through footprints, food response, temperature, displaced foliage, and nesting traces.
first20Seconds: Offer two foods; one vanishes in a cold shimmer and the habitat thermometer drops.
desktopControls: Click evidence, choose care cards, orbit the habitat.
mobileControls: Tap traces and large care icons.
xrControls: Ray-scan traces and place care tokens; no handling required.
comfort: Fixed enclosure; optional snap rotation; captions and visualized sound direction.
signatureVisual: Frost, bent grass, footprints, and floating food reveal the path of a creature whose body never appears.
whyDifferent: Discovery and welfare share one evidence loop: every care action is also a testable hypothesis.
nearestComparable: Strange Horticulture and creature-care simulations.
originalityRisk: Low - the unseen-care premise creates a strong and unusual loop.
hackathonScope: One enclosure, two species, five trace types, and three care actions.
technicalRisk: The ending needs a satisfying outline reveal without breaking the invisible-creature promise.

title: Origami Rescue
hook: Fold distant places together to rescue citizens.
fantasy: Fold a paper world so stranded rooftops, roads, rivers, and shelters become adjacent in three dimensions.
first20Seconds: Lift one marked crease until a rooftop touches a fire escape and the first citizen crosses.
desktopControls: Click a crease, drag its angle, pin, then run.
mobileControls: Tap a crease, use a large angle slider, tap pin.
xrControls: Lift a crease handle and pin it, with one-handed ray fallback.
comfort: Seated tabletop; authored slow folds; no head intersections; stepped reduced-motion mode.
signatureVisual: A paper city rises along one crisp fold until a stranded rooftop meets a safe road.
whyDifferent: Legal folds rewrite a rescue path graph and finish as a commemorative route pattern, rather than matching a folded picture.
nearestComparable: Paper Trail and folding-topology puzzles.
originalityRisk: Medium - paper folding is established; rescue routing and layered adjacency must lead.
hackathonScope: One page, three creases, four regions, and two citizens.
technicalRisk: Use rigid hinged panels and a small graph instead of deformable paper physics.

title: Size Thief
hook: Steal size from objects to change their purpose.
fantasy: Siphon a finite amount of scale between objects until doors, keys, tools, and routes cross useful thresholds.
first20Seconds: Drain size from an enormous key into a tiny door; the key becomes usable as the door becomes a passage.
desktopControls: Click donor and receiver, wheel amount, hold to transfer.
mobileControls: Tap the pair and drag a central amount slider.
xrControls: Ray-select the pair and squeeze a pump handle.
comfort: Fixed room or miniature; gradual bounded scaling; never scale the camera or world.
signatureVisual: A brass siphon shrinks a giant key while a tiny doorway grows into a human-sized passage.
whyDifferent: Total size is conserved between donor and receiver, creating budget tradeoffs instead of perspective-based arbitrary scaling.
nearestComparable: Superliminal and scale-manipulation puzzle games.
originalityRisk: High - scale puzzles are recognizable; conservation accounting must be visible immediately.
hackathonScope: One room, five objects, three thresholds, and two solutions.
technicalRisk: Authored scale tiers must preserve collision and interaction affordances.
